---
title: "Ghazals for an Absent Friend"
subtitle: "Two poems by Karim Bendaoud."
description: "Meditations on distance and longing in the classical ghazal form."
author: "Mehdi Khribch"
date: "2026-02-14"
categories: [Poetry]
image: https://picsum.photos/seed/rrb-ghazals/1200/800
image-alt: "Placeholder"
---

The ghazal—that ancient Arabic form of connected, autonomous couplets—has long been a vessel for intimate address across unbridgeable distances. Karim Bendaoud, who spent a decade in Marrakech before relocating to Brussels, returns to this form to speak to a childhood friend from whom he has been geographically and circumstantially separated. What emerges is not so much nostalgia as a kind of radiant faithfulness: the beloved absence that paradoxically confirms connection. Bendaoud employs the classical ghazal's capacity to collapse time and space while maintaining its formally broken form.

### Ghazal: The Letter Never Sent

<div class="rrb-poem">

Dear friend, I am writing to you from a city where no one knows your name.
The streets here smell of pine, not cedar. Still I find you everywhere.

The café where we first read that terrible translation of Rumi—
it was destroyed in a flood. The floodwaters carried away your bookmark.

I did not save you. I could not. Each day I fail you differently.
This too is a form of faithfulness, I think. Though you are not here to think it.

The olive seller on the avenue—he reminds me of your uncle.
I buy from him and do not explain. What is there to say to a stranger?

You wrote that solitude is a river. I wrote back that you were wrong.
Now I live beside one, and every day the water says your name.

The gardener here grows roses the color of old arguments.
I do not ask her how. Some things should remain unexplained.

When winter comes, the city whites itself out like a photograph.
All the faces I did not memorize become suddenly precious.

Dear friend, I am writing to you in pencil so I can erase it.
Some forms of honesty require a way out.

</div>

### Ghazal: Inheritance

<div class="rrb-poem">

You left me the blue cup. It arrived wrapped in newspaper from Fez.
I have not drunk from it. This is how I know you are still alive.

My mother says this is foolishness. She drinks from every surface.
But she did not know you when you were young and could be known.

The cup holds light differently than other cups. You knew this when you left it.
You knew I would spend years learning what you already understood.

There is a spider living in the cupboard where I keep it.
I think you would approve. You always said: let things find their own shelter.

Last month I dreamed you were in the market, selling figs.
When I approached you turned away. In the dream I understood this was correct.

The blue cup sits in darkness. Above it, my reflection in the cupboard door.
We are not the same person anymore. The cup remembers both of us.

You cannot write. I cannot ask. The spider has built a new room in the handle.
This is how distance becomes domestic. This is how love persists.

I will not drink from the cup. But sometimes I hold it to the window.
The light that passes through it lands on my palm like a small, impossible hand.

</div>

---

*Karim Bendaoud teaches Arabic poetry at the Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam. He has published three collections in French and Arabic, and his work has appeared in *Boundary 2*, *Asymptote*, and various anthologies. He is currently working on a manuscript about diaspora and classical form.*
